title: Chapter 17: The Inside
content: The ring weighed more than it should have.

Two carats, ethically sourced — or so the certificate claimed — set in rose gold with a delicate pavé band that caught the light of every boardroom chandelier Jane passed beneath. She wore it everywhere. Edward insisted. Not with words, exactly, but with the way his hand would find hers at dinner, his thumb pressing the band against her finger like sealing a contract. The ring was a statement. Jane understood statements. She made a few of her own.
By day, she was the perfect fiancée.

She sat beside Edward at board meetings, her tablet open, her posture immaculate, taking notes that no one would ever read. She charmed investors over lunches where the wine cost more than her mother had earned in a week. She reviewed collection previews with Edward, nodding at fabric swatches and silhouettes, and when he said "our shared vision," she smiled and let the words settle over her like a sheer veil — translucent, suffocating, useful.
Because Edward was giving her access.

It started with production schedules — she'd asked about lead times for the spring collection, and he'd handed over the full manufacturing calendar without hesitation. Then marketing budgets. Then vendor contracts. Then, last Tuesday, a thin folder marked *Confidential — IPO Preliminary* that he left on the nightstand between them like a gift. Jane had read it cover to cover while he showered, the steam curling under the bathroom door, and by the time he emerged, she'd memorized the key figures and returned the folder to its place.
He trusted her. Or he was testing her. With Edward, the two were indistinguishable.
By night, she was someone else entirely.

The archive was cold after hours. The climate-control system that preserved the decades-old pattern books and fabric samples ran constantly, pumping dry, refrigerated air through the stacks, and Jane learned to bring a cardigan even in summer. The hum of the servers in the adjacent room was a low, ceaseless drone — a vibration she felt in her sternum before she heard it with her ears, like standing too close to a transformer. The fluorescent lights buzzed overhead, casting everything in that sickly institutional pallor that made food look unappetized and skin look dead.

She logged into the maintenance terminal at 11:47 PM, precisely when the automated backup cycle ran. The screen filled with scrolling log entries — timestamps, process IDs, memory allocations. Boring. Invisible. Exactly as planned.
She scrolled until she found it:

```
[MAINT-0042] Disk cleanup node 7 — redundant cache cleared.
Old partition: /archive/m1973/bertha/config
Retention flag: OVERRIDE. Proceed? Y/N
```
Jane typed: `Y`
The response came twenty seconds later, embedded in a routine memory diagnostic:

```
[MEM-DIAG] Sector 14 verbose output:
You're getting comfortable up there. Good. Comfortable people make mistakes.
But comfortable people also get access. How much does he trust you now?
```
Jane smiled. Her fingers moved quickly:

```
[MEM-DIAG] Sector 14 response:
IPO preliminary docs. Vendor contracts. Full budget authority for spring line.
He left the IPO folder on the nightstand. Like a test I'm supposed to pass.
```
The reply came slower this time, the cursor blinking in the dark:

```
[MEM-DIAG] Sector 14 verbose:
Then pass it. But keep copies. Everything you touch, duplicate.
We need the architecture. I'll teach you the map.
```
The map was more labyrinth than blueprint.

Bertha walked her through it over four nights, each session disguised as a routine diagnostic cycle. The financial architecture of Thornfield's greenwashing operation was staggering in its complexity — a paper trail so enormous and so deliberately convoluted that any forensic accountant would need months and a subpoena just to identify the entry points.

Four shell companies. Malta first — a holding entity called Aegean Textiles Ltd. that invoiced Thornfield for "sustainable fiber certification" services that had never been performed. Luxembourg next: Verdant Capital S.à r.l., which received the Malta payments and funneled them into a web of investment accounts. Cayman Islands after that — Blue Reef Holdings, the exit point where the cleaned money re-entered the Rochester family trust as "diversified returns."
But it was the fourth company that made Jane's stomach clench.

Seychelles. A corporation called Mahé Wellness Consulting Ltd., registered three months ago. It was the newest node on the map, the freshest wound, and Bertha couldn't access its internal ledgers from the server room — the encryption was military-grade, separate from the Thornfield mainframe.

"Physical access," Bertha wrote. "That's what you're for. The digital trail ends at Mahé. Whatever they're hiding there, it's on paper or on drives that never touch this network. You need to find it."

Jane's job was simple to describe and impossible to execute casually: collect the pieces, document everything, and do it all while wearing a two-carat ring and smiling at the man who'd built the labyrinth.

She started carrying a micro-SD card in the lining of her handbag — a slim little thing, no bigger than her fingernail, onto which she copied every file Edward gave her access to. She photographed documents with her phone and deleted the images from the camera roll, transferring them to the card each night. She kept a separate notebook — handwritten, analog, untraceable — in which she sketched the architecture as Bertha described it, the four shell companies connected by arrows that curved and doubled back like blood vessels.
She was building the case from the inside. And the inside was terrifying.
The board meeting was on Thursday.

Jane sat in her usual seat beside Edward, tablet poised, the rose gold of her ring catching the light from the conference room's floor-to-ceiling windows. Beyond the glass, the Loire stretched silver and placid under a pale sky. The room smelled of espresso and fresh-cut lilies — Edward always had lilies at board meetings, their thick sweetness cloying at the back of Jane's throat.
The agenda was routine until Monsieur Delacroix, the longest-serving board member, cleared his throat and adjusted his cufflinks.

"Sustainability metrics," he said, tapping the printed report before him. "The Q3 figures show a forty-two percent reduction in water usage across our Indian production facilities. Impressive. But I'd like to understand the methodology. How are we measuring this?"

The CFO, a trim man named Pichon with the permanently tightened expression of someone who'd swallowed a lemon wedge whole, launched into a prepared response about sensor integration and automated reporting systems. Jane let him talk. She watched the numbers scroll on her tablet — the public sustainability report, open on the left side of her screen. On the right, the production budget Edward had given her three days ago.
The numbers didn't match.

They couldn't match. The public report claimed forty-two percent water reduction. The production budget showed three new dye vats installed at the Coimbatore facility in the same quarter — vats that consumed water at the old rate. The reduction was calculated against a baseline that had been silently revised upward, making the decrease appear dramatic when the actual consumption had held steady.
Jane's pulse quickened. She felt the ring press against the tablet's edge, a small hard reminder of everything she was risking.
She waited for Pichon to finish. Then, in the silence that followed, she spoke.

"If I might ask a question?" She kept her voice light, curious — the eager fiancée, learning the business. She looked at Delacroix, not Edward. "The public report cites a forty-two percent reduction in water usage. But the production budget shows new dye vats installed in Coimbatore this quarter, which would require — what? Approximately two hundred thousand liters per cycle at standard capacity?"
Pichon's mouth opened. Nothing came out.

Jane continued, still mild, still looking at Delacroix: "The sustainability report uses a 2021 baseline for comparison. Should these numbers be different if we're measuring against current capacity rather than a revised baseline?"
The room was very quiet. Jane could hear the air conditioning cycling, the distant clatter of dishes from the catering kitchen, the soft leather creak of Pichon's chair as he shifted his weight.
"Should they be different?" Delacroix repeated, looking at Pichon.
Pichon fumbled. "The baseline methodology is — it's industry standard to — the vats are part of a transitional — we're implementing a phased —"

"We'll review the methodology," Edward said smoothly, cutting through the stammer. His eyes found Jane. Not angry. Not yet. Something between admiration and alarm — the look of a man who'd handed someone a scalpel and was only now realizing how close she was to the artery. "Excellent eye, darling."
Jane smiled. "I want to understand our shared vision."
Helen's message arrived at 6:14 AM London time, which meant she'd been awake all night.

Jane found it on her personal phone, the one Edward didn't know about — a cheap prepaid she kept in the lining of her handbag alongside the micro-SD card. She read it in the bathroom, perched on the edge of the tub with the shower running to cover the sound, the steam thick and warm against her face.

*"Story's dead at the paper. Kate killed it this afternoon. 'Too much legal risk. Not enough confirmed sources.' Her words. I sat in her office for forty minutes making my case and she wouldn't budge. I'm devastated but I'm not done. Working it on my own time now — public records, company filings, the documents you sent. There's enough there if I can verify the shell structure. Malta I can confirm through open-source. Luxembourg is tougher. Cayman is a black box."*
Then, a second message:
*"Be careful. If they're watching email, they're watching you. Delete this."*

Jane deleted it. Then she sat in the steam and breathed and thought about Helen alone in her London flat with a wall of printouts and a dead story and the kind of stubbornness that got people killed or got them Pulitzers — sometimes both.
Helen was the outside. Jane was the inside. Bertha was the ghost in the machine. Three women, three positions, one case. It had to be enough.

The Seychelles company was the key. Jane felt it in her bones — that particular certainty that came not from evidence but from instinct, the same instinct that had told her to attend the board meeting with two sets of numbers in her head.

She found the thread on a Tuesday night in the archive, the cold seeping through her cardigan as she scrolled through Edward's personal expense records — a cache he'd accidentally left accessible when he'd granted her IPO-level clearance. It was sloppy. He was getting sloppy, or he was getting confident, and with Edward those were the same thing.
Mahé Wellness Consulting Ltd. — the Seychelles entity. It funneled €14,000 per month to a French firm called *Cabinet Fournier*, registered in Lyon. Cabinet Fournier employed exactly one person.
Dr. Antoine Fournier. Edward's personal psychiatrist.

The man who had diagnosed Bertha Mason with acute dissociative disorder andrecommended long-term residential care. The man whose clinical opinion had consigned her to the basement of Thornfield and the ministrations of Grace Poole. He was on the payroll — not as a physician, not as a consultant to the company's wellness program, but as a line item buried three levels deep in a shell company's disbursements.
"Wellness consultant." The irony was bilious.

Jane's hands were steady as she photographed the ledger entries. Her mind was not. This was hush money. This was proof. Fournier hadn't just diagnosed Bertha — he'd been retained, paid, and presumably directed. The diagnosis wasn't medicine. It was architecture. A system designed to disappear a woman and make it look like care.
That night, she logged into the maintenance terminal at 11:47 and typed:

```
[MEM-DIAG] Sector 14 alert:
Found it. Mahé Wellness -> Cabinet Fournier. €14K/month.
Still active. He's still being paid.
```
The response came faster than usual, as if Bertha had been waiting:

```
[MEM-DIAG] Sector 14 verbose:
Fournier. I knew he was paid. I didn't know it was still active.
He's still on the books. That means the system is still in place.
Not just for me. For whoever comes next.
```
Jane stared at the blinking cursor. Her fingers found the keys:

```
[MEM-DIAG] Sector 14 response:
There is no next. We end this.
```
She typed the words with conviction. Her hands shook anyway.

Because she'd gone back to the Seychelles registration after she'd found Fournier's name. She'd checked the incorporation date three times, thinking she'd misread it, thinking the timezone conversion had thrown something off, thinking any explanation other than the one that was settling into her chest like ice water.
Mahé Wellness Consulting Ltd. was registered three months ago.
After Bertha was already locked away. After the diagnosis. After the basement. After Grace Poole and the server room and all of it.

They weren't maintaining the system for Bertha. The system was already running for her — Malta, Luxembourg, Cayman, those were the old architecture, built to disappear one woman. Mahé was new. Mahé was expansion. Mahé was the second facility in a franchise that hadn't finished building its patient roster yet.

Jane sat in the cold archive with the servers humming in her bones and the ring heavy on her left hand, and she understood — with the crystalline, nauseating clarity of someone who has just stepped onto a trap and heard the mechanism engage — that Edward wasn't just keeping one woman locked up.
He was institutionalizing the practice.

The Seychelles company was three months old. The engagement was five weeks old. The ring was two weeks old. The timeline wasn't coincidental. It was architectural. The same mind that had built the labyrinth for Bertha was building a new wing, and Jane had walked through the front door, smiled at the blueprints, and said *I do*.
She was the next patient. And the reception had already been furnished.
